content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Don't make the same mistake I almost did. Go watch The Sheep Detectives!

**Score:** 454
**Body:** I almost skipped *The Sheep Detectives* in theaters because, based on the title and marketing, it just didn't seem like something I'd enjoy. It looked like one of those movies I'd eventually stream if I had nothing else to watch.

I couldn't have been more wrong.

Without spoiling anything, this movie completely surprised me. It's funny, clever, emotional when it needs to be, and genuinely engaging from start to finish. It has the kind of storytelling that reminds you why going to the movies is worth it.

If you're a movie lover, do yourself a favor and don't let the title or your first impression stop you. I almost ignored it, and I would've missed one of my favorite movie experiences of the year.

For me, *The Sheep Detectives* is easily one of the most underrated yet one of the best movies of 2026, and it's absolutely worth watching on the big screen while you still can.

Has anyone else gone in with low expectations and ended up loving it?

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I thought it would be funny but I couldn’t stop thinking about the one sheep who’s witnessed every horrible thing that’s happened to his friends and he can’t forget it and can’t even talk about it with anyone.
  - I couldn’t get over the baby winter lamb. 
    - I can’t get over the carnival brutality.
  - I liked how the film framed that as a disability and really followed through on that idea
  - Read the book! It is awesome. As soon as I heard about the movie I knew it had to be based on the book.  I loved it - now I need to see the movie. 
2. It’s kind of the perfect intro to murder mysteries for elementary aged kiddos but it’s also a genuinely affecting adult film. 

I also thought they perfectly calibrated keeping the human characters more one-note and flat, so that the sheep themselves had more of the emotional depth and resonance, instead of using the sheep as silly add-ons to a human story. 

And the visual effects were truly stunning. There were in fact times I couldn’t quite tell if I was looking at a CGI sheep or a real one, and considering just how many shots have CGI sheep, that’s quite a feat. 
3. I took my kids to see it, I went in with no expectations and was prepared to mentally check out like I do for a lot of modern kid-friendly movies. I was absolutely charmed by it and agree that it’s worth seeing in the theater to give your full attention to solving the mystery.
4. Bah
  - Ram
5. More or less - the family got me to watch it.

It's a solid family-friendly Knives Out.  All-time classic, probably not, but certainly worth a watch for anyone who likes murder mysteries.

## 2. Cheesiest campy absurd 80s movies

**Score:** 23
**Body:** I really like stuff like Secret Admirer or Mannequin both are romance but the suggestions don’t need to be though romance is preferred. I just like how over the top, eyerolling absurd, wacky and camp these are. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Night of the Comet is a camp horror classic.
2. The Adventures of Buckaroo Banzai Across the Eighth Dimension

Big Trouble in Little China
3. Weird Science 
4. Earth Girls Are Easy -1989 with Jeff Goldblum and Geena Davis. 
5. Space Camp. Cant get campier than a camp that's out of this world
6. Better Off Dead

Weekend at Bernie’s 

License to Drive

Adventures in Babysitting
7. Flash Gordon (1980)
8. The Stuff

Killer klowns from outer space 
9. Splash (but the writing is really good)
10. Teen witch lol its so cringe

## 3. Looking for movies where the sequels are just as good... or even better than the original

**Score:** 20
**Body:** I'm looking for franchises where the quality doesn't drop after the first movie. ones that genuinely hold up or even surpass the original.

Any genre is fine... action, thriller, sci-fi, horror, animation, anything. Any country/language works. What are your suggestions?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Lord of the rings
2. empire strikes back? 
3. Riddick

Mad Max
4. The films Yojimbo and Sanjuro
5. Shrek 2 is the best in the franchise. 
6. Back to the Future 
7. Thor: Ragnarok is the best of the 3

Bourne Ultimatum
8. Star Trek 2: Wrath of Khan was a far better movie than ST1.

Maybe an unpopular take but... Star Wars: Empire Strikes Back was (to me) much better written than the first movie, had a more meaty plot and had some real character tension.

Terminator 2 for sure.

Alien was one of the best horror movies of all time, but Aliens was one of the best action movies of all time.

Godfather the original was fantastic, a classic. But Godfather 2 was a whole 'nother level.
9. Aliens and Terminator 2

## 4. Looking for films that feel like a fever dream

**Score:** 17
**Body:** Looking for something similar to After Hours, Eyes Wide Shut or The Game. Just want to be like 🫨 by the end of it. Ideally it takes place in the span of one night, but not necessary. Thanks! :)

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. mulholland drive - or anything by David Lynch
2. Jacob's ladder 
3. Beyond the Black Rainbow. Absolutely one of the most beautiful films I've seen, but the plot + that aesthetic is an absolute fever dream. The fever dream aspect is likely at least partly intentional, but I think it still applies here. 

And that Synth heavy OST is baller.
4. Mandy 
5. Mother! 
6. Paprika

Its anime. I'm not an animal watcher but this is top 3 favorite movies ever
7. The Exterminating Angel (1962) - I LOVE this film. The concept is an aristocratic dinner party is held at a mansion and afterwards, for some strange reason no one seems to be able to leave...

Picnic at Hanging Rock (1975) - unreal cinematography, great atmosphere building mystery. 

Zazie Dans le Metro (1960) - I struggle to describe this one but I really enjoyed it. It transports the viewer to 1960's Paris in a way you didn't know you needed. 
8. Black Bear (2020) and I’m Thinking of Ending Things (2020) 
9. Weapons, Bugonia, Waking Life, Vanilla Sky, Death Becomes Her, Brazil, I'm a Cyborg But That's Ok
10. Mad God.

  
Total mindfuck of a movie. 

## 5. Beach themed movies with an edge.

**Score:** 17
**Body:** Not “Beaches” or “Nights in Rodanthe” for example. 
We are looking for beach or coastal themed movies with a twist. 

We’ve hit “Cape Fear” tonight. 
“The Piano” is on the agenda. 

Any other ideas? 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Point Break
2. The Beach
3. The Talented Mr. Ripley has that sunny Italian coast vibe hiding a very dark core
4. Jaws
5. Spring Breakers
6. Blue Crush will make you want to live in a swimsuit and surf every day. 

Not really beachy exactly, but Double Jeopardy is mostly coastal setting if you’re okay with something sort of Pacific Northwest sailboat murder/disappearance. 
7. Psycho Beach Party
8. the lost boys!!
9. A Perfect Getaway- 2 couples meet in Hawaii on their Honeymoon and decide to travel together
10. Sleeping with the Enemy

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Which actor played a villain role too well for comfort?

**Score:** 2923
**Body:** Ralph Fiennes as Amon Göth in Schindler's List.

The only thing that kept going through my head was not the great acting by Fiennes, it was “Take this piece of shit away from my screen”.

I have seen many villains on movies, but none were creepier than Amon Göth. And the reason why Fiennes' acting was so creepy is because of how casual he makes evil. Not because he was the most entertaining villain ever, but because he was the most realistic one. Fiennes acted as Göth so casually that you can totally forget that he is actually an actor. One of the few actors who actually acts "too well for comfort".

Fiennes also acts as Göth without hesitation. He never gives hints that he knows that we are watching him and he does not try to entertain us. He acts so realistically that it becomes extremely creepy. As with his speech acting, his body language deserves equal attention. His posture, his voice, his casual way of carrying himself all of that becomes contrasting to what he does in reality.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Jake Gyllenhaal in Nightcrawler. I found him to be a pretty accurate depiction of a narcissist & opportunist around the corporate/media world

![gif](giphy|tyttpH0csEhCKS2FIrK)
2. ![gif](giphy|LXP19BrVaOOgE)
3. https://preview.redd.it/10z6yxk546ch1.png?width=318&format=png&auto=webp&s=480316ad060bfb6f89d74b2f3e1e51679ed061dc


4. ![gif](giphy|2eHFEYBoJO3mw)
5. Joaquim Phoenix as Commodus in Gladiator.

Joaquim brought a great range to the character, from pathetic to threatening, jealous to almost likeable

![gif](giphy|5ncqEwQBqUnXq)
6. Kevin Bacon as Sean Nokes in Sleepers

https://preview.redd.it/0lp9k2xq66ch1.jpeg?width=466&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=45d067cf3b74e410db8d9a74481fb77d44cbd29f
7. The real Amon Göth was so horrible Spielberg had to tone down his character because he thought nobody would believe someone could be so evil 
  - The real Goth got relieved of his position as Camp Commandant because the SS (as in the most die hard Nazis there are) thought he was violating regulations about how to treat prisoners.

He went too far for the fucking NAZIS.
8. ![gif](giphy|3ov9jFb1R1nzm0OA6c)
9. Jason Issacs plays exceptional villains but I have never wanted to see a man get what is coming to him more than William Tavington.

https://i.redd.it/efbobpgrs6ch1.gif

## 2. Name an onscreen comedic duo that compliment blew away your expectations with their onscreen chemistry and banter. I'll go first:

**Score:** 2367
**Body:** Russell Crowe and Ryan Gosling in The Nice Guys (2016).

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. https://preview.redd.it/7hiz7xiia7ch1.jpeg?width=1359&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=2e790ca02833b48bf999aa4b07bf0ce483c4eb67

Brendan Gleeson and Colin Farrell - In Bruges
2. Gosling could have chemistry with a rock.

Dude is an amazing actor.
3. ![gif](giphy|3oz8xTl6sGKbuRPDDW)
4. ![gif](giphy|11iRfaoc7AefjW)
5. ![gif](giphy|1dLOO8yAP222vaVLsC)
6. ![gif](giphy|rKDPfrc60EXU4)
7. Old School- Gibson & Glover
8. Woody and Wesley. "It's pretty, it's so pretty."

![gif](giphy|gw3CxaRrNjXLvtMk)
9. RDJ and Val Kilmer in Kiss Kiss Bang Bang
10. https://i.redd.it/ip5l27xkd7ch1.gif

## 3. The Count of Monte Cristo (2002) is one of the most epic revenge films

**Score:** 1213
**Body:** I’ve read the book many times and am aware as to how much the movie deviates from the source material.

But man this movie is so entertaining! Watched this so much back in the day, it’s crazy. I think I wore out the dvd, lol 

The prison escape always used to get me so hyped and the way Edmund takes down his enemies is so satisfying.

The scenes with him and Richard Harris were always my favorite scenes.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Is that Cavill face in the background? 
  - Yup!!!

He plays Albert, Mondegos son.
  - Yep! It's also really funny catching him in *Stardust* (2007)

https://preview.redd.it/7idl25uq8ach1.jpeg?width=750&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=f6714eb45e78f630ce1802e51b0d1be03f553ee1
2. "The Count of Monty Crisco... by Alexadry Dumbass...Dumbass?"
  - One of the best parts of shawshank lol
3. One of the best stories ever told, check out the Masterpiece series
  - And then check out the Wishbone episode!
    - ![gif](giphy|fDPWSVvLVZtza)
4. This is one of my favorite movies! 
  - https://preview.redd.it/z0z7qdwp6ach1.jpeg?width=3424&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=0ddf4b6db6ce7567cc6a900a4d91a8730fc53ec0

Same! I hate Guy Pearce in this movie so much it’s unreal.

Amazing actor! But I despise his character here so much

## 4. What's a movie scene you watched at way too young of an age that absolutely traumatized you? For me it was Poltergeist and i know you know kinda win on this

**Score:** 538

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I just rewatched this along with the 2015 remake. Such a great film, so tragic what happened to the two young stars. R.I.P. 
  - The clown. That stuck with me for awhile. I’m 57 now and it’s still sticking with me so you do the math. 
    - That gd clown is the reason I can still, to this day, make the long jump from my door to my bed. I'm 45 and STILL can't do walking near a bed in a dark room. (I sleep with the TV on and there is at least one low watt light on at night in my house) Poltergeist and Child's Play fucked my childhood up. (Sideyeing you teddy ruxpin)
2. For me it was watching Robocop at the theater with my friends at age 11. 

Murphy’s death is still hard to watch.
  - Robocop for me too but watched it alone in my parents room when I was 5. The toxic waste scene got me
3. I saw jaws when I was 6. Still went swimming but I was looking for sharks in every pool, lake, pond, river and bathtub I was in. 
4. I watched Aliens when I was like 3 and it scared the shit out of me, but the one that traumatized me deep deep down, was the shoe in Rodger Rabbit.
5. If it counts as a short film, the Thriller music video. Saw it at the age of 5 and it terrified me. Funny thing is, I’m obsessed with horror now (movies, books, games, you name it). That said, whenever I hear Thriller come on, I break out in a cold sweat to this day. I refuse to even watch the video. 
6. “I know you know kinda win on this”

What?
7. Pink Floyd: The Wall

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. It is an insult to culture and humanity that so few movies are public domain.

**Score:** 173
**Body:** I am trying to watch 8 1/2 and i havent found any place to watch it on good quality and with subtitles. A movie from the 1963 A classic that has shape cinema since and there is no way to watch it freely. A movie like this should be on Youtube. Another classic: Citizen Kane. It is a hard film to watch freely and legaly when it should be the first result on Youtube. Who is the company that holds the rights of this movies for probably a pennies instead of releasing it to the public? This copyright law doesn´t benefit nobody. If Orson Welles and Federico Fellini were alive today they would love that everybody could watch their film. But some random company take the rights of their movies (nobody knows why or how) and we are bound to follow this random ass laws Disney paid millions of dollars to make. It is a miracle that other countries like Russia dont have this dumb idea of intelectual property and you could watch any tarkosvky movie just searching it in Youtube. 

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. You could try Kanopy, free with a library card if your local library belongs to it. My library belongs to Beamafilm, and it has 8 1/2 and it's catalogue is nowhere near as big as Kanopy 
  - Here for the Kanopy shoutout. 
    - I feel like people don't give it a shot. I have an easier time finding something worth watching on Kanopy than Netflix.
2. I believe the same but sadly, working on the field of film preservation, I learned that without a financial support, movies can hardly go to public. We have to remember that a movie isn't a mp4 file, but a film that have to be kept in an archive, and restored when needed. It's extremely costly and copyright can bring a source of revenues. 
3. The length is definitely overboard but I think it's a good thing that the creator of a film is guaranteed to be compensated for it until they die, maybe it should be inherited so the creator can choose whether they want their film to go into public domain when they die or they can pass it on to someone else
4. *It is a miracle that other countries like Russia dont have this dumb idea of intelectual property*

Yes, it’s quite the miracle in the way that Russia has managed to steal from creatives world over just as they managed to steal from folks right at home
5. I joined a really big plex server awhile back and pay like 10 bucks a month to be a part of it.

I know it is at least in the morally gray area of the piracy side of things, but in the modern world, digital ownership isn't ownership. For far too many movies, there is no way to own it.

And I adhere to the idea that if you can't own it, you can't steal it. 

Now I do have a massive personal film collection of movies that I own, but I refuse to be held hostage by digital licensing.
  - I’m kind of amazed there are people who will pay money to have other people pirate stuff for them. 
    - With the price of hard drive space nowadays maybe it’s a good idea. 
    - I feel like I am mostly paying for the server space to be honest. I mean, the server I pay 10 bucks a month to has like 100,000 movies, 25,000 TV shows, and a huge chunk of them are 4k.

The cost of the server space alone for that many movies has to be in the tens of thousands of dollars plus the technical know how for upkeep.

I'll happily pay for someone else to deal with that.

## 2. King hu movies and Wuxia genre

**Score:** 23
**Body:** I recently watched Wong Kar Wai's Ashes of Time and really enjoyed it. There was something about the elegance and grace of the movie that reminded me of Crouching Tiger Hidden Dragon. 

I am interested in exploring more of what the Wuxia genre has to offer and I keep seeing the movies of King Hu appearing as recommendations. I see that he has got quite a few movies. To those who are fans of his work, which movies are the best place to start? What makes his movies stand out to you?

I have to admit that I am not a huge fan of martial arts movies in general but I am getting the sense that King Hu's movies have a certain artistic flare and philosophical depth behind them that may make them appeal to a wider audience. Curious to learn whether this is the case?

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I always suggest starting with *Come Drink With Me*, *Dragon Inn* and *A Touch of Zen* for starting King Hu / Wuxia in general. Hu's work definitely has an "operatic" feel and has strong dramatic tension.

This is contrast to his contemporary Chang Cheh whose films are blood soaked, hypermasculine adrenaline pieces. *Golden Swallow* is a sequel to *Come Drink With Me* but definitely stands a lone as its own piece. *The One-Armed Swordsman* is Chang's crowning achievement but also check out *The Heroic Ones*.
  - I’d personally say Five Deadly Venoms is his crowning achievement. Five Element Ninjas is also a great watch and a fan favorite.
2. Dragon Inn is usually what people would recommend because it's a tight-paced action film but honestly, I would recommend A Touch Of Zen, if you're looking for anything that's aesthetically rich, has philosophical and religious themes and has unconventional storytelling / story structures. It's an absolute monster of a 3-hour-long wuxia / political thriller, and probably my favorite Chinese period film of all time.
  - *Love* the ending to this film. Definitely not something I would've expected from a Western narrative point of view. I also really enjoy how the presence of a spirit is (usually) implied with recurring nature motifs, giving everything this big sense of grandeur, and then eventually some of them just walk into rooms to talk to human characters as things seem to escalate.
3. King Hu's work do have a lot of depth and he created innovative visual styles and techniques that were hugely influential.  The King of Wuxia (2022) is a two-part film which spans 216 minutes and explores his artistic process, and cultural impact (mostly in the first part) and his life (mostly in the second.)

As you say you're interested in exploring more about what Wuixa has to offer, I'll note that Hu, more than, say Ang Lee in Crouching Tiger, is working deeply within a Chinese cultural and artistic and philosophical context, and some of what makes it his work interesting might not be as easily legible as with other Asian wuxia film makers.  For example Tsui Hark's work is more in dialogue with Western films, and his Green Snake has themes like religious zealotry and gender relationships that are pretty legible even if you lack cultural context.    
  
I'm not saying you have to be from a Buddhist culture and have read Strange Tales from a Chinese Studio by Qing dynasty writer Pu Songling to appreciate A Touch of Zen, but if you happen to be, you'd get a bit more out of it.    
   
The traditional entry point would be Dragon Inn.  It's more straightforward, and even if you aren't a huge fan of martial arts films, the action is in service of the characters and narrative, not just for it's own pleasures.   Plus, as a bonus, you get context to later  watch Goodbye Dragon Inn, which is a classic of slow cinema and the Taiwanese New Wave.

A Touch of Zen might be Hu's finest work, but I suspect it's a bit more elusive to many people outside his cultural context.  It's still amazing, and essential cinema, but not everyone likes the experience of staring in wonder but feeling that they are clearly missing some things.
4. Great comments. When ready, you should watch Zhang Yimou's movies in this genre, Hero, House of Flying Daggers, Curse of the Golden Flower and Shadow. Zhang's use of color in these movies, you'll flip your lid. 
5. You’re about to go down a rabbit hole. I hope you’re ready.

I think of the great kung fu movies as three distinctive eras. That may not be 100% accurate as I’m not an authority. The first is the 60’s-70’. That’s the golden harvest era and the Shaw bros movies that Tarantino is always on about.

Then you have the late 80’s, which is probably the best from a technical choreography and filmmaking standpoint. This is the Tsui Hark, Jackie Chan and Sammo hung era. 

Then you get into the 2000’s with Ang Lee’s people-floating-on-wires stuff. Although I think it’s a stretch to even consider crouching tiger a kung fu movie. It’s more like a period fantasy with elements of kung fu. There’s also a lot of good stuff to come out of this era. Jet Li, Donnie Yen. Zhang Yimou’s melodramas are really good.
6. It’s interesting because Ashes of Time is usually regarded as an atypical wuxia movie, and very different from Kinghu’s works. 

Many people know a lot about Kinghu here. Also, if you feel like less kongfu and more philosophical depth. I will suggest that, among modern martial art genre, try Reign of Assasins (2010). If you feel more ambitious to know martial arts film in the last decade China, you may watch those directed and written by Xu Haofeng , whose novels also influenced Wong Kar-Wei to make The Grandmasters(2013) and  Chen Laige’s Monk Comes Down the Mountain(2015). But they are all very very distinct from each other. 


PS just pass the most recent film (2023) of Xu Haofeng. Poor reviews. 
7. He stands out to me against other wuxia directors for the elegance and grace you mention, the spiritual bent his films often take, and his eye for visuals (immaculate production design and shot composition).  

Not that there's any continuity to them but I would just watch his feature films in order starting at Come Drink With Me. The progression from that to Dragon Inn to A Touch of Zen is enjoyable to observe, and the next 4 are all great in their own ways. I personally haven't explored his work post 1979 apart from Swordsman which I did not like.

## 3. A Woman Under the Influence: I have a few questions

**Score:** 21
**Body:** I watched A Woman Under the Influence yesterday and I haven't been able to stop thinking about it. It was extremely compelling and I didn't expect to like it as much as I did. But I have a few questions.

What illness did Mabel really have? She seems confused and disoriented all the time and she forgets things pretty much every time. Is that due to her illness or because of the electroshock therapy she possibly endured in the past? If I had to guess, I'd say she's probably bipolar due to her erratic moods that we see throughout the film. I also thought it was interesting how people in the film, especially her parents, reacted to her. They seem so confused and don't know what to do with her. Has she been ill throughout her life or did she become mentally ill from postpartum depression? That might explain the confusion and why her husband constantly asks her to snap out of it. But people being so used to the way she acts probably points to the fact that she may have always been that way. 

I know these aren't questions really aren't relevant to the plot and the movie is mostly about how people treat mabel in relation to her illness but I thought it would be interesting to consider. 

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. >“Mabel is not crazy, she’s unusual. She’s not crazy, so don’t say she’s crazy.”

I think the entire point is NOT to diagnose her, but rather to embrace her, that that is what love is. To love into the absurdities and the pain. And Cassavetes constructs a film where the audience can do that. We love Mabel...and others (the characters) should too. The greatest sadness is WHEN they commit her, put her under medical definition. Cassavetes is very big on the idea of loving people with their flaws, even because of their flaws. He wants to bring out the human condition - hence his extreme raw-feeling, improvisational-styled realism. He felt that this was essential to loving life itself. Often though some of his films can read as confessions to his own failings towards his own wife (Gena Rowlands), even as they glorify her.

I think though we are meant to project all sorts of "misfitting" onto her, our own and of others, and feel that intense empathy. It can't be a single thing.
  - This is some great analysis.
  - Really appreciate this comment and its expanded my view on the film. Thank u!
    - Have you read the short story "The Yellow Wallpaper"? I think it's a good companion piece to the film, despite how long ago it was written. 
2. The filmmaker Derek Cianfrance (*Blue Valentine*, *Place Beyond the Pines*) names this as one of his favorite movies. A quote from him: 

> The thing about A Woman Under The Influence, too, is that when I first saw it, I was pretty sure Gena Rowlands was crazy in the movie—that it was a movie about a crazy woman. But I’ve watched that movie a hundred times, and the last time I watched it, I realized she was the only sane person in the movie. Everyone else was crazy. I love that about that film, that it’s actually alive in that way. It has the ability to change, based on who I am as a viewer. There’s an openness to it. You can participate in it. It’s like a friend almost, that movie. I feel like it’s a living, breathing organism. It’s not set in stone.
  - I agree a lot with this. This movie hasn't left my mind yet and I've struggled with film analysis and understanding movies before, but this felt like the first film that I could actually "converse" with. I found myself taking notes and writing my own thoughts about it after I finished watching, which I basically never do.
    - >I found myself taking notes and writing my own thoughts about it after I finished watching, which I basically never do.

That's so beautiful to hear. You may enjoy some of Cassavetes other great films. I think A Woman Under The Influence is his best, and Gena's best performance (possibly the greatest actress performance I've seen), but films like Killing of a Chinese Bookie or Opening Night are also marvels in similar spirit. His later Love Streams also explores some of the same "crazy but not crazy" territory, also with Gena Rowlands.

Another spirit animal of AWUTI in my opinion is Possession (1981), though a divorce made as horror film, if you haven't seen it. Also which a spectacular on-the-edge female performance. (though not everyone's cup of tea).
      - All great suggestions, thank you
      - *Opening Night* is my favorite Cassavetes/Rowlands. 

I had seen *All About My Mother* (Almodóvar) first, so it was one of those times when I got the homage before the original thing. Same shocking event to get the story in motion. Rowlands is among the women thanked in the inscription at the end of the Almodóvar movie. 
  - I had never heard of this movie but that quote has compelled me to put it on my list.

## 4. What makes something cinematic? Can a painting be cinematic? Can a book?

**Score:** 3
**Body:** For the past couple of hours, I have been really interested in this topic. I witnessed a debate the other day between two people where one described a painting being “cinematic” and the other saying that doesn’t make any sense because it isn’t a moving picture, it can’t be cinematic. 

I’ve been watching a couple of videos on this matter and I can’t seem to figure out if any of them are right or if they’re both right. I guess the dictionary definition of “cinematic” is “relating to or suggestive of motion pictures.” But honestly, that doesn’t tell me anything. I would love some insight on this.

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. To me, anything that deviates from reality tends towards "cinematic."

Long/short focal lengths feel "cinematic" because we typically don't view the world through telephoto lenses or through quite that wide of a POV.

Composed shots feel "cinematic" because people typically aren't standing around in compositionally-pleasing poses for our aesthetic enjoyment. 

Camera movements feel "cinematic" because we typically aren't moving and turning our heads in interesting ways.

Intentional lighting feels "cinematic" because the vast majority of lighting is practical and not aestethic.

Production design feels "cinematic" because the modern world tends to be colorless and drab as fuck.

If all of this actually serves the story, then it heightens the "cinematic" feeling.

But to answer your question, **it's not so much that paintings can feel "cinematic," it's more that cinema is borrowing ideas and aesthetic concepts from traditional artforms like painting, while adding additional elements like motion and sound.** Painters have been experimenting with composition, color, and lighting for centuries.
2. I mean, I don’t know that there’s a definitive explanation of what is “cinematic” and particularly how it’s applied to other art forms that aren’t cinema.

But I will personally say I think to me, “cinematic” means two things:


1. It’s evocative of both story and emotion. Cinema is at its core usually preoccupied with telling stories, and conjuring emotional experiences in its audience. So for that reason, paintings, music, books can easily fit that criteria.


I would say books can very easily fit that definition, and so usually when a book is described as “cinematic”, it’s because the prose is focused more on visual explanation and exterior action, rather than internal monologue.

Cinema can’t easily conjure internal monologue in the way books can, and so to me when a book is “cinematic”, say like the source novel of No Country For Old Men, it’s because the focus is on descriptions of what is directly said and done, which is essentially how screenplays are written.

Similarly, if a piece of music is deemed “cinematic”, I would say it generally has fewer lyrics and less of an imposing rhythm or melody, and thus is evocative without being prescriptive.
In other words, it leaves space to imagine images and emotions, much in the same way a good book can.

2. Having a level of craft and expertise in regards to the visual language is the other criteria I’d use. Cinema is and has always been the most expensive art form on the planet. It doesn’t cost an artist $80m to write a novel, or a band $300m to make an album. But movies almost always cost incredible sums of money.

Because of that, cinema often (though not always) has a level of care and craft on screen that represents the best of the art form, because movies have the budget to go to places and dress and light them well, whereas something even like most television shows do not necessarily do. That’s why the Westerns of John Ford are infinitely more “cinematic” than a TV of a similar era like Gunsmoke.

So when something is truly “cinematic”, to me it is utilising the kind of budget and time (that comes with budget) to perfectly express itself. Now, the perfect visual or technical encapsulation of a story/emotion doesn’t necessarily *need* a big budget, and there’s plenty of films throughout time that have nailed something cinematic without costing hardly anything.

But I do think they are generally the exceptions, and that when we’re using the term “cinematic”, it’s usually a by-word for saying something is “premium” or “high quality”.
